speaker: interviewer
text: Voros, tape 4. Irene, do you feel that any of the decisions in your life have been influenced by your Shoah experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think that my-- my conversion to Catholicism had partially to do with my interest in-- in the Christian religion because it-- the Jewish religion was not supplied to me by my parents. But the reason I chose Catholicism-- not only 'cause my husband was Catholic, but primarily because when I took my Montessori training, which was in the '61, I came in contact with a catechist who talked about the importance of Catholics looking back to their Jewish roots. And I knew that when I raise my children I wanted to give them the Christianity that I believed in and the Jewishness, which was what I was. And this gave me an opportunity, I felt, you know, to do both. I know that roots are very important to me in all my teaching that I do. I-- I focus on where people come from, the children's ancestors, spirituality. I encourage parents to give their children something, you know, whatever it is because of my-- my experience of-- of that. And I think leave taking is so important to me that I-- I almost make a ritual out of-- when children leave my school, we have a regular ritual of saying goodbye. And I emphasize that a person can never disappear out of another person's life without somehow [SOBBING] marking it. And I think that that comes from the ways in which these things happen to me, you know, in a negative, in a negative way. I, I have a, I have a real sense of the importance of the-- of the human being and not so much well-- while I think ethnicity and religion is very interesting, I don't think it should cut people apart from each other. It should not be a reason [SOBBING] for people to hate each other or-- or ignore each other. And I think that's part of-- of my experience that I'm trying to rectify. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Off hand, that's what I can think of. I'm sure there's more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have your experiences-- being in hiding, living under false papers-- affected how you raised your three children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The most important thing that I stress to the children is compassion. I will never talk about any person anywhere in a disparaging way. I will not let them do it if they brought up something about why is so-and-so like this. I'd always come up with a reason. Oh, I'm sure it's because this or that in an attempt to try to explain to them that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we cannot, we cannot mark people into a particular category, to separate us ourselves from them, that-- that we need to see in what way we are alike. And [SIGHS] In this way I, I-- I'm hoping to actually I say in my handbook, in my school, that I'm trying to prepare the children that I teach to be citizens of the world, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so that I'm not just doing this for my own children, but other people's children. It's really important to me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In any other way that I raise my children, I have not tried to direct my children as to who they are friends with or who they marry. And this is just totally off the subject, but I-- I-- I wanted to-- to mention this earlier. When I was very-- when I first came to America, I had never seen a black person, I've never seen anyone of color. And I was very fascinated by them, to the point where I stared at them and my uncle in New York said don't do that. But I remember swinging on the swing and saying to myself, I wonder how these people feel. And then I remember swinging back and forth a few more times and saying, they feel like Jews. They feel different. And [SOBBING] that affected what-- what I tried to do. I was not successful at it. But I actually tried to teach in what is called disadvantaged areas. I prepared myself to teach black children because I felt they needed an extra hand up, but I did it at a time when there was not enough preparation so that I-- I had no way to really understand their-- their culture and help them, but-- I feel that that affected, you know, that my-- my past experience affected my desire to-- to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you done anything to instill or foster a Jewish identity in your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not other than to remind them of my Jewishness. When my mother died and she didn't do the Passover dinners anymore, then I would do it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel at a real loss there because I didn't have my husband's support for any religion, not just Jewish religion. He didn't support religion. But since, since I'm living by myself, I have invited them over to dinner. And I somehow feel [SOBBING] they have empathy with me as their mother. [CRYING] They have some empathy with Jews, but I can't make the connection with them. I just-- [SOBBING] You know, when I tell them the stories, which I do very infrequently, I feel like they don't want to hear it, I'm imposing on them. [SOB] And yet they tell me they do want hear it. So it may be just my perception, I don't know what it is. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's something I can't pass on to them because I haven't got it to give them. You know, I-- I was not raised in a religious home. I did not raise them in a religious home. But even with Catholicism, they moved away from that. I mean, it wouldn't have mattered. Kids do move away from what they were raised with. I think ultimately they understand my past and the impact on-- on their lives, as much as I can expect it to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any messages or lessons you want to leave for your children and for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've said some of it. I-- I believe that [SIGHS] we need to, to focus on-- on each other's humanity and on a god that loves us all and created us all. And that the difference is not anything except that people [INAUDIBLE]. There are no differences between people. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] That to use religion as a way of fighting against your fellow man is just a big cop out, it's been misused. It is not what is dividing us. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I-- I do have a fear about the future. I am tremendously appalled that there are shades of Nazism in the-- in the United States and everywhere else in the country. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel a little helpless other than what I'm doing now to do anything about it. I don't know what-- you know, I don't know exactly what to do except [SIGHS] I feel that, I feel we cannot forget what happened. But I feel that we must forgive what happened. I don't know how to fight evil. Evil was a big-- big issue that I-- I want not to look at. But I-- I I know it exists because of people's false perceptions. And I'm hoping that, that something like this and other things that unite people, rather than, you know, divide them, will help overcome what-- what I hope is the minority in the uneducated faction of our society. I do think that education is part of the answer. I also think that when people are prejudiced, then they strike out against one another. As we know, that Nazism came about because of the economic insecurity of the country. When people feel secure about themselves and-- and about their lives, they don't need somebody else to strike out against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess it behooves all of us to make the world-- sounds like preaching, but to make the world a better place for everyone, so that no one has to choose someone else as, as their scapegoat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think that's about it. Thank you very, very much, Irene.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my grandfather on my mother's side-- Isadore Beinhacker. And it was taken probably sometime-- 1920, 1930. I got the picture from my uncle, it was a small snapshot, and it was enlarged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my grandmother on my mother's side, Ernestine Beinhacker. It was taken in Austria, same time between 1920, 1930s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, same way. It was a small snapshot enlarged. And I got it from my uncle. This is my mother and my father in Vienna, Austria in September 1935. It's their wedding picture, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- my mother brought this from Hungary. This is a picture of me on July 9, 1930, 1940. And I'm three and a half years old. And I'm in that boarding home where I lived with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary on Margaret island.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come about getting this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother brought this. This is a picture of my father in Budapest, Hungary-- 1940, between 1940 and 1944. And my dad might have brought this over, my mother might have from Hungary. This is a picture of my father taken in Hungary after the war, after 1945. And either my mother or father brought this over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary, in Budapest. This is a picture of my mother taken in the artist loft of Mr. Szekely, who got us the false papers before 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother brought this out when she came. It was all rolled up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungary, Budapest. This is a picture of me in [HUNGARIAN] that Mr. Szekely made in about 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary. [LAUGHS] This is a picture of me with the cat that kept me company in 1946 in Budapest, Hungary. This is a picture of me at my uncle's house when we arrived in 1947 in July, in New York, Queens, New York. This picture is in the early '80s. It's my mother in the red dress, then myself, my son [? Bundy, ?] my son Lorant, and Christy, taken in my mother's apartment in Chicago.
